coredata.dc:title: A facile and rapid detection of Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid using N,S-co-functionalized carbon dots: A dual-mode 'on-off-on' fluorescent sensor
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Nelson, D. James
Gowthaman, N.S.K.
Sinduja, Bharathi
Sethuraman, M.G.
coredata.dc:description: This study reports the successful synthesis of nitrogen- and sulfur-co-functionalized carbon dots (N,S-CDs) using citric acid and L-cysteine through a microwave-assisted hydrothermal process. These N,S-CDs were employed for the quantification of dichromate ions (Cr(VI)) and ascorbic acid (AA) through a fluorescence “on-off-on” detection method. The N,S-CDs displayed deep blue fluorescence, with an emission peak at 420 nm upon excitation at 349 nm. The presence of Cr(VI) significantly quenched the fluorescence intensity, enabling its selective detection over a linear range of 1–40 μM with a limit of detection of 58 nM. The addition of AA restored the fluorescence intensity by reducing Cr(VI) to Cr(III), allowing for AA detection within a dynamic range of 5–90 μM with a detection limit of 180 nM. The fluorescence quenching by Cr(VI) involved both the inner filter effect (IFE) and static interactions and the IFE was reversed upon the introduction of AA, which restored the fluorescence. This method demonstrated excellent sensitivity and selectivity for detecting Cr(VI) and AA, with successful application in the analysis of real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-and-S-co-functionalized carbon dots
Cr(VI)/Cr(III) redox probe
Ascorbic acid
'On-Off-On' fluorescent sensor
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for Cr(VI) and AA determination
Characterization of N,S-CDs by HR-TEM, XRD, Raman spectroscopy and XPS techniques
Spectral characterization of N,S-CDs by UV–vis, PL, and FT-IR spectroscopy
Selectivity study of Cr(VI) and detection mechanism analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the authorities of The Gandhigram Rural Institute for their encouragement and support. N.S.K. Gowthaman would like to thank SRM TRP Engineering College, India, for their financial support, vide number SRM/TRP/RI/005.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image (inset: particle size distribution), (b) SAED pattern, (c) XRD pattern and (d) Raman spectrum of N,S-CDs.
XPS (a) survey and high-resolution spectra of the (b) C1s, (c) O1s, (d) N1s, and (e) S2p for N,S-CDs.

(a) Absorbance spectrum of N,S-CDs, (b) Fluorescence excitation (red curve) and emission spectra recorded at excitation wavelengths from 280 to 410 nm in 10 nm intervals, (c) pH-dependant emission spectra of N,S-CDs (λex = 349 nm), spanning pH 2–12, and (d) FT-IR spectrum of N,S-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs upon each 1 μM addition of Cr(VI) (1–40 μM) at pH 6. (b) Calibration plot of PL intensity vs. Cr(VI) concentration. (c) Stern-Volmer plot for fluorescence quenching vs. Cr(VI) concentration. (d) Double logarithmic plot of quenching efficiency vs. Cr(VI) concentration.

(a) Cr(VI) absorbance spectrum overlaps with the excitation and emission spectra of N,S-CDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of N,S-CDs at increasing concentrations of Cr(VI) (1–40 μM). Fluorescence lifetime decay profiles of N,S-CDs (c) before and (d) after Cr(VI) addition.

(a) Fluorescence emission studies of the N,S-CDs/Cr(VI) system upon AA addition (5–90 μM) at pH 6, (b) calibration plot of fluorescence intensity vs. AA concentration, and (c) absorbance spectra of N,S-CDs/Cr(VI) upon AA addtion (5–90 μM).
Graphical representation of the fluorescence ‘On-Off-On’ mechanism for Cr(VI) and AA detection using N,S-CDs.
Comparison of the present method with other reported methods for Cr(VI) detection.
Comparison with other fluorescent probes for AA detection.
Cr(VI) detection in various aqueous samples.

head.ce:title: A facile and rapid detection of Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid using N,S-co-functionalized carbon dots: A dual-mode 'on-off-on' fluorescent sensor

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Advanced Energy Materials, SRM TRP Engineering College

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports the successful synthesis of nitrogen- and sulfur-co-functionalized carbon dots (N,S-CDs) using citric acid and L-cysteine through a microwave-assisted hydrothermal process. These N,S-CDs were employed for the quantification of dichromate ions (Cr(VI)) and ascorbic acid (AA) through a fluorescence “on-off-on” detection method. The N,S-CDs displayed deep blue fluorescence, with an emission peak at 420 nm upon excitation at 349 nm. The presence of Cr(VI) significantly quenched the fluorescence intensity, enabling its selective detection over a linear range of 1–40 μM with a limit of detection of 58 nM. The addition of AA restored the fluorescence intensity by reducing Cr(VI) to Cr(III), allowing for AA detection within a dynamic range of 5–90 μM with a detection limit of 180 nM. The fluorescence quenching by Cr(VI) involved both the inner filter effect (IFE) and static interactions and the IFE was reversed upon the introduction of AA, which restored the fluorescence. This method demonstrated excellent sensitivity and selectivity for detecting Cr(VI) and AA, with successful application in the analysis of real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium in its trivalent form (Cr(III)) is a vital trace element that supports glucose and lipid metabolism. Most Cr(III) species exhibit low solubility, limited absorption by cellular tissues, and minimal bioaccumulation potential [1]. Cr(III) is considered as essential in trace amounts for glucose and lipid metabolism and is often used as a dietary supplement, though its necessity remains debated [2–4]. In contrast, hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) species are highly mobile, readily absorbed by cellular tissues, and can easily penetrate biological membranes, making them particularly hazardous to the environment [5,6]. Cr(VI) is widely employed in industries, such as paints, pigments, metal plating, leather tanning, steel manufacturing, wood preservation, catalysis, and nanoporous photocatalysis [7], consequently leading to chromium contamination in industrial wastewater. In recent decades, the environmental persistence of Cr(VI) has significantly increased due to its extensive industrial usage [8,9]. The World Health Organization (WHO) has set the permissible Cr(VI) limit in drinking water at 50 μg/L (∼1 μM), underscoring the necessity for highly sensitive and rapid detection methods for trace-level Cr(VI) monitoring [10].

Ascorbic acid (AA), vitamin C, is an essential water-soluble nutrient with crucial biological and chemical functions. It plays critical roles in neural functions, collagen biosynthesis, wound healing, and tissue integrity maintenance [11–13]. As a potent antioxidant, vitamin C helps to prevent conditions such as osteoarthritis, cataracts, cardiovascular diseases, Alzheimer's disease, dementia, cancer, diabetes, hypercholesterolemia, hypertension, obesity, and age-related macular degeneration [14–17]. Insufficient AA levels can lead to scurvy, characterized by symptoms such as weakness, fatigue, swollen gums, haemorrhaging, joint pain, and delayed wound healing [18]. Conversely, excessive vitamin C intake may interfere with medications, including anticoagulants and chemotherapy drugs, potentially causing adverse effects. Overconsumption can also lead to gastrointestinal discomfort, such as diarrhoea, nausea, and abdominal cramps, and may contribute to kidney stone formation, particularly in individuals with pre-existing kidney conditions [19,20]. As a result, the food and pharmaceutical industries have increasingly emphasized developing precise and user-friendly methods for AA quantification.

To date, various analytical techniques—including colorimetry, fluorometry, chromatography, atomic absorption spectroscopy, capillary electrophoresis, and electrochemical methods have been explored for detecting Cr(VI) and AA [21–30]. Among these, fluorescence-based methods have attracted considerable attention due to their simplicity, high sensitivity, and real-time detection capability. Cr(VI) sensing typically involves redox reactions or fluorescence quenching mechanisms such as the inner filter effect (IFE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) or energy transfer processes [31–33]. Despite the successful development of various fluorescence nanoprobes for Cr(VI) detection, limitations such as insufficient sensitivity, poor selectivity, high preparation costs, complicated quantification procedures, and complex sample preparation continue to restrict their broader application. AA plays a pivotal role in redox-mediated fluorescence recovery mechanisms across diverse sensing platforms. For example, in the CuCNs-CoOOH system, CoOOH (containing Co³⁺) is reduced to Co²⁺ by AA, resulting in enhanced fluorescence [34]. In the Cu2+-o-phenylenediamine system, AA is oxidized to dehydro-AA while Cu²⁺ is reduced to Cu⁺, thereby modulating the fluorescence signal [35]. Similarly, in the GSHCuNCs-Cr(VI) system, Cr(VI) quenches fluorescence via the IFE, and AA restores it by reducing Cr(VI) to Cr(III) [36]. Under mild acidic conditions, AA can effectively reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III) while being oxidized to dehydro-AA [31]. These redox interactions highlight the critical role of ascorbic acid not only as a reducing agent but also as a modulator of fluorescence signals, underscoring its utility in the design of sensitive and selective fluorescence-based sensing systems for Cr(VI) and related analytes.

Carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant interest as fluorescent probes owing to their outstanding optical properties, high chemical stability, low cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility and these attributes make them highly suitable for diverse applications, including photocatalysis, energy conversion, sensing, and bioimaging applications [31,37–43]. CDs have demonstrated significant potential for sensing various metal ions (e.g., Fe(III), Cu(II), and Hg(II)) and molecular targets (e.g., dopamine (DA), curcumin and choline) [39,44]. Despite their potential, challenges persist in achieving CDs with high quantum yield (QY). Heteroatom doping, such as incorporating nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus, has been shown to enhance the fluorescence performance of CDs effectively [31,40,45,46]. Among the various synthetic approaches, including pyrolysis, hydrothermal, and solid-state methods, microwave-assisted synthesis offers unique advantages such as shorter reaction times, improved QY, better control over particle properties, and environmental friendliness [41,43], further advancing their development for sensing applications.

In this study, nitrogen- and sulfur-functionalized CDs (N,S-CDs) were prepared through a microwave-assisted hydrothermal approach, utilizing citric acid and L-cysteine as precursors . A fluorescence-based sensor employing N,S-CDs as fluorescent probe was designed for the detection of Cr(VI) and AA. The fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs in the presence of Cr(VI) resulted from the combined effects of the IFE and electrostatic interactions. With the addition of AA, partial reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) occurred, leading to significant fluorescence recovery in the N,S-CDs/Cr(VI) system. This fluorescence recovery enabled the design of a highly sensitive and selective fluorescent probe for the dual determination of Cr(VI) and AA. The proposed sensor offered a broader detection range and lower detection limits compared to previously reported CDs-based optical sensors. Additionally, the synthesis method for N,S-CDs was straightforward, convenient, and time-efficient, further enhancing its potential for practical applications.

All the chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade and their detailed information is provided in the supporting information (SI). The synthesis of N,S-CDs was adopted by modifying the previously reported procedure [47]. Briefly, a mixture of 1.0 g of citric acid and 0.3 g of L-cysteine was dissolved in 10 mL of double-distilled water and irradiated with microwave energy at 280 W for 8 min. After the evaporation of water, an orange-colored gel was obtained, which was then purified using column chromatography with a 1:20 ethanol-dichloromethane mixture. The purified orange solid of N,S-CDs was kept in the dark and subsequently used for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for Cr(VI) and AA determination
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence-based detection of Cr(VI) and AA using N,S-CDs probe was carried out as follows: 2 mL of N,S-CDs solution (25 μg/mL) was prepared using 0.5 mM phosphate buffer (pH 6) as the solvent. Subsequently, 1 μL aliquots of a 2 mM Cr(VI) solution were added incrementally to achieve final concentrations ranging from 1 to 40 μM (in 1 μM increments), and the fluorescence spectrum was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 349 nm. The characteristic emission of N,S-CDs at 420 nm was quenched upon Cr(VI) addition, exhibiting a “turn-off” fluorescence response. A calibration plot was made by correlating emission intensity at 420 nm against the concentrations of Cr(VI), enabling the quantification of Cr(VI) levels. Fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs was most efficient at pH 6, with a reaction time of 2 min achieving stable and maximum quenching.

The quenched N,S-CDs/Cr(VI) solution was subsequently employed as a fluorescence “turn-on” probe for AA detection. Incremental additions of 5 μM AA (ranging from 5 to 90 μM) were introduced into the N,S-CDs/Cr(VI) solution, followed by a 2 min incubation. The emission spectra of the resulting solution were monitored at an excitation of 349 nm. The interaction between AA and Cr(VI) led to the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) and the simultaneous oxidation of AA into dehydroascorbic acid (dehydro-AA), resulting in the restoration of fluorescence. A calibration plot was established by correlating the recovered fluorescence intensity at 420 nm with AA concentrations, facilitating the quantification of unknown AA levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N,S-CDs by HR-TEM, XRD, Raman spectroscopy and XPS techniques
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 1
a shows the HR-TEM image of N,S-CDs, revealing a uniform particle distribution with an average diameter of 13.1 nm, as shown in the inset. Fig. 1 b presents the hazy SAED pattern, suggesting the partial crystalline nature of the N,S-CDs [48], which originates from the aromatic, carbon-rich parts of the precursors that undergo thermal carbonization and partial graphitization, leading to the formation of sp2-hybridized graphitic domains through π-π stacking and the alignment of conjugated carbon structures. In contrast, the amorphous structure is derived from non-aromatic or aliphatic components, incompletely carbonized organic moieties, and surface functional groups.

The structural properties of the synthesized N,S-CDs were examined using X-ray diffraction (XRD). A broad peak from the XRD pattern appeared in the range of 18°–36° along with a sharp peak at 28.1°, corresponding to the (002) crystallographic planes of the graphitic surface (Fig. 1 c) [44,48]. These results confirm that the N,S-CDs possess a partially crystalline structure with amorphous characteristics. The interplanar spacing (d-spacing) of the N,S-CDs was computed using Bragg’s equation, yielding a value of 3.2 Å for the (002) plane. For comparison, the ideal graphitic plane typically has a D-spacing of 3.3 Å [49]. The slightly reduced interlayer distance in the N,S-CDs was attributed to nitrogen doping within the graphitic lattice. Furthermore, the crystallite size was determined to be 5.67 nm for the (002) planes using the Scherrer equation.

Raman spectroscopy was utilized to further explore the structural features of the N,S-CDs. Fig. 1 d presents the Raman spectrum, exhibiting prominent peaks at 1355 cm⁻¹ (D-band) and 1562 cm⁻¹ (G-band), which correspond to the structural defects in the graphitic domains and the in-plane vibrations of sp² carbon atoms, respectively [43]. Additionally, two weak peaks were observed at 2703 cm−1 and 2945 cm-1, corresponding to the 2D and the D + G bands, respectively. The sharpened 2D band suggests increased graphitic ordering and the presence of mono-layer graphene-like domains, while the D + G band at 2945 cm-1 indicates reduced structural defects and enhanced sp2 hybridization in the carbon framework [50]. The intensity ratio of the D band to the G band (ID/IG) was measured to be 0.83, indicating a well-graphitized structure with moderate disorder in the N,S-CDs framework. The low structural disorder arises from minimal incorporation of nitrogen into the graphitic core, maintaining the structural integrity.

The surface chemical composition and functional groups of as-synthesized N,S-CDs were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2
a depicts XPS survey spectrum, confirming the incorporation of nitrogen and sulfur, alongside carbon and oxygen in the CDs framework. The atomic composition of N,S-CDs (C: 49.1 %, O: 40.3 %, N: 7.5 %, and S: 3.1 %) suggests high oxygen content, likely from surface functional groups, and successful heteroatom doping. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum (Fig. 2 b) revealed distinct peaks corresponding to sp²/sp³-C, CO/CN, C=N, C=O/C=S, and COOH groups, with binding energies ranging from 284.2 to 287.9 eV [33,45]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2 c) displayed peaks at 530.6, 531.6, 532.6, and 533.5 eV, attributed to C=O, CO, -COOH, and S=O groups, respectively [45–47].

The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2 d) displayed peaks corresponding to pyridinic-N (399.1 eV), amine (399.6 eV), pyrrolic-N (400.4 eV), and pyridine-N-oxide (401.3 eV) functionalities [45]. Additionally, the deconvoluted S2p spectrum (Fig. 2 e) revealed peaks attributed to C=S at 163.8 eV, sulfoxide (S-O) at 167.1 eV, and sulfone (S=O) at 168.4 eV [32]. These results indicate the successful integration of nitrogen and sulfur atoms into both the surface and structural framework of the N,S-CDs. Furthermore, the incorporation of NH₂, COOH, and -OH functional groups enhances the hydrophilicity of N,S-CDs, thereby improving their adaptability for various applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral characterization of N,S-CDs by UV–vis, PL, and FT-IR spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 3
a presents the absorbance spectrum of N,S-CDs, displaying a prominent peak at 345 nm, attributed to n-π* transitions associated with surface functional groups, and a shoulder at 240 nm, corresponding to π-π* transitions associated with delocalised π electrons system within the graphitic framework [39,51]. Fig. 3 b displays the excitation and emission spectra of N,S-CDs, with the maximum excitation (λₑₓ) observed at 349 nm. Emission spectra were recorded for excitation wavelengths from 280 to 410 nm in 10 nm increments, revealing an emission maximum (λₑₘ) at 420 nm when excited at 349 nm. The excitation-independent emission suggests uniform emitting states and high structural stability of the successfully synthesized N,S-CDs [51].

The influence of ionic strength on N,S-CDs emission was evaluated by varying NaCl concentrations from 0 to 1 M at pH 7. As depicted in Fig.S1a, the fluorescence intensity was maintained even in the presence of 1 M NaCl, suggesting that the high ionic strength enhanced colloidal stability by mitigating electrostatic repulsions and preventing aggregation [31]. Additionally, the photostability of N,S-CDs was assessed under continuous UV irradiation for 24 h (Fig.S1b), revealing negligible fluorescence degradation, thereby confirming their strong resistance to photobleaching. This exceptional stability is attributed to effective surface passivation, which prevents aggregation and protects the N,S-CDs from photodegradation [46].

Fig. 3 c illustrates the pH-sensitive emission nature of N,S-CDs, showing an increase in fluorescence intensity accompanied by a blue shift as the pH varied from 2 to 12. This highlights the pH-responsive nature of N,S-CDs, emphasizing their suitability for sensing applications in different environmental conditions. Fig.S1c shows the variation in emission intensity of N,S-CDs (λₑₓ = 349 nm) across a pH range of 2 to 12. Under highly acidic conditions (pH 2), the N,S-CDs exhibited the emission maximum at 442 nm with the lowest emission intensity. As the pH increased from 2 to 6, the emission intensity significantly enhanced, accompanied by a blueshift of λₑₘ to 420 nm. Beyond pH 6, the emission intensity remained relatively stable, and λₑₘ showed no further significant shift. The reduced fluorescence intensity and redshift in acidic conditions can be attributed to the protonation of nitrogen-containing functionalities, which disrupts the π-electron delocalization within the N,S-CDs' structure. Conversely, the increase in pH deprotonates these functional groups, restoring electron delocalization and enhancing fluorescence intensity [52,53]. These findings highlight the critical role of pH in influencing the fluorescence behavior of N,S-CDs by altering the electronic environment of their surface. Fig.S1d displays images of N,S-CDs solutions under UV irradiation across the pH range of 2 to 12. The QY was determined to be 49.3 % (SI) [39]. The FT-IR spectrum of N,S-CDs, presented in Fig. 3 d, provides detailed insights into their functional groups. A broad band at 3567 cm⁻¹ shows the presence of -NH₂ and -OH groups. A strong absorption band at 1710 cm⁻¹ is assigned to COOH groups, while a sharp peak at 1636 cm⁻¹ is indicative of C=C/C=N stretching, coupled with CONH₂ groups [54,55]. Additionally, a shoulder band at 1523 cm⁻¹ corresponds to NH bending, and a weak band at 1398 cm⁻¹ is attributed to CC/CN in the graphitic framework of the CDs [40]. Vibrations associated with -OH and -NH₂ groups are evident from bands at 1300 cm⁻¹ and 1246 cm⁻¹. Further evidence of nitrogen functional groups includes CN stretching at 1181 cm⁻¹, 1165 cm⁻¹, and 1070 cm⁻¹, suggesting the presence of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N at the CDs skeleton [40]. The weak peaks at 1043 cm⁻¹ (S=O) and 1109 cm⁻¹ (CO) indicate the presence of sulfone and alcoholic groups, respectively. Notably, C=S stretching bands were observed in the range of 600 - 800 cm⁻¹ [40,55]. The aforementioned FT-IR analysis confirms the incorporation of diverse functional groups, highlighting the complex and functionalized nature of the synthesized N,S-CDs.

Under optimized conditions, the emission of N,S-CDs was measured at an excitation of 349 nm (λex) after the stepwise addition of Cr(VI). As shown in Fig. 4
a, the emission intensity of the N,S-CDs progressively diminished with increasing Cr(VI) concentrations from 1 to 40 μM. Moreover, the emission maximum shifted from 420 to 428 nm (redshift), likely due to the interactions between Cr(VI) and N,S-CDs. The calibration plot in Fig. 4 b reveals a distinct linear correlation between the emission intensity and the concentration of Cr(VI). The detection limit was calculated as 5.79087 × 10⁻⁸ M ≈ 0.058 μM (S/N = 3.3), which is significantly lower than the WHO recommended permissible limit for Cr(VI) in drinking water (50 μg/L or 0.231 μM) [7]. The Stern-Volmer plot (Fig. 4 c) demonstrated a linear correlation between the quenching efficiency (F0/F) with lower concentrations of Cr(VI). The linear equation, F0/F = 7.465 × 104[Cr(VI)] + 0.977, demonstrated a strong correlation (R² = 0.992), where F0 and F correspond to the emission intensities of the N,S-CDs at 420 nm before and after the addition of Cr(VI), respectively [31]. Additionally, a double logarithmic plot of the relative fluorescence quenching ((F0−F)/F versus [Cr(VI)] revealed a binding site number of 1.18 and a binding constant of 5.76 × 105 M−1 (Fig. 4 d). Table 1 provides the comparative analysis of the linear detection range and detection limit of the N,S-CDs probe against other reported fluorescence sensors for Cr(VI) determination [29–31,41,46,51,56,57]. The LOD achieved in this study is significantly lower, underscoring the sensor's high sensitivity and its potential for practical applications in Cr(VI) detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity study of Cr(VI) and detection mechanism analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the proposed N,S-CDs sensor for Cr(VI) detection was evaluated by analyzing potential interference from various common and heavy metal ions. As shown in Fig.S2, even at elevated concentrations, these metal ions caused negligible fluorescence quenching, confirming the high specificity of this sensor toward Cr(VI). This selective detection is attributed to the IFE induced specifically by Cr(VI), which does not occur significantly with other metal ions [29,31,41]. Further support for this high selectivity is provided by the anti-interference study shown in Fig.S3. The PL spectra of N,S-CDs (5 μg/mL) remain largely unaffected in the presence of various metal ions (0.2 mM each), as evidenced by the minimal change in PL intensity (blue curve) compared to the initial fluorescence signal. However, the introduction of 10 μM Cr(VI) in the presence of all other metal ions resulted in marked fluorescence quenching, clearly demonstrating the probe’s excellent selectivity for Cr(VI) detection.

Fig. 5
a illustrates the distinct absorbance peaks of Cr(VI) at 358 and 255 nm, which overlap with the excitation peaks of N,S-CDs at 244 and 349 nm. This overlap induces the fluorescence quenching via the IFE, enabling the selective detection of Cr(VI) amongst all other ions [41]. To confirm the quenching mechanism, the absorption spectra of N,S-CDs were measured at varying Cr(VI) concentrations from 1 to 40 μM (Fig. 5 b). Upon the addition of Cr(VI), minimal shifts in the N,S-CDs absorbance indicated that a static quenching mechanism also contributed to the observed fluorescence quenching. These interactions are likely due to the oxidizing nature of Cr(VI) [57]. Fluorescence decay measurements provided further insights into the quenching mechanism. Fig. 5 c illustrates that the fluorescence lifetime of N,S-CDs remains largely unchanged upon Cr(VI) addition, confirming that the fluorescence quenching mechanism is primarily governed by the inner filter effect [29]. The fluorescence lifetime analysis of N,S-CDs revealed two distinct lifetime components, with the longer-live component (τ2= 14.34 ns) contributing 96.62 % to the overall decay, indicating the presence of stable emissive states, likely associated with both surface and core-related electronic transitions. Upon Cr(VI) interaction, a significant decrease in the short-lived component (τ1= 6.81 ns to 3.05 ns) and a slight reduction in the average lifetime (from 13.82 ns to 13.46 ns) suggest a minor contribution from non-radiative energy transfer or static interactions. The higher contribution of the long-lived component (τ2) in both cases implies that Cr(VI) primarily interacts with surface states, the core states remain largely unaffected [31,51]. These results confirm that IFE is the major quenching mechanism, with static interaction playing only a minimal role, further supporting the potential of N,S-CDs as an effective fluorescence-based probe for Cr(VI) detection.

Building upon previously reported redox-responsive systems, in the N,S-CDs-Cr(VI) platform demonstrates a reliable fluorescence recovery mechanism triggered by AA. In this system, AA reduces Cr(VI) to Cr(III), thereby mitigating the IFE and resulting in a significant restoration of fluorescence intensity. The interference studies presented in Fig.S2 demonstrate that Cr(III) has minimal influence on the N,S-CDs emission. The residual Cr(VI) concentration in the solution exhibits a direct correlation with the AA concentration. This reduction process decreases the IFE, leading to the restoration or retention of fluorescence in the N,S-CDs/Cr(VI) system. Furthermore, the initial Cr(VI) concentration in the analytical solution plays a crucial role in determining both the extent of fluorescence recovery and the sensitivity of AA detection. To optimize the dynamic range for AA quantification, a higher Cr(VI) concentration was selected to achieve significant fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, 40 μM of Cr(VI) was selected as it quenched nearly 90 % of the fluorescence intensity, providing a well-defined range for AA detection. This substantial quenching improves sensitivity and broadens the dynamic range for monitoring fluorescence recovery during the AA addition. Moreover, the strong quenching at 40 μM Cr(VI) minimizes background interference, thereby improving the accuracy and reliability of AA quantification. Fig. 6
a presents the fluorescence spectra of the N,S-CDs/Cr(VI) system with AA concentrations from 1 to 90 μM at pH 6, showing a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity with increasing AA levels. Fig. 6 b depicts a well-defined linear correlation between fluorescence recovery efficiency (F/F0) and low AA concentration, where F0 and F denote the fluorescence intensities of the N,S-CDs/Cr(VI) system at 420 nm before and after AA addition, respectively. The linear equation derived from the calibration plot is F/F0 = 3.589 × 10⁻⁶ [AA] M⁻¹ + 61.527, with a determination coefficient (R²) of 0.9954. The detection limit for AA was determined to be 0.18 μM (S/N = 3.3). Table 2 provides a comparative analysis of the synthesized N,S-CDs with previously reported fluorescence probes for AA detection, emphasizing their broader linear range and enhanced sensitivity [20,29–31,34,36,46,56].

To assess the selectivity of AA detection using the N,S-CDs/Cr(VI) system, the influence of various amino acids and biologically relevant compounds was examined (Fig.S4). The results demonstrate that the most potent interferents exert minimal influence on AA detection. Notably, DA exhibited a significant effect, likely due to its quinoid structure, which can further interact with the N,S-CDs and contribute to minimal fluorescence suppression. The anti-interference capability was further validated through PL studies (Fig.S5), where the PL spectra of the N,S-CDs/Cr(VI) system remained largely unchanged upon the addition of 0.5 mM of various biomolecules. However, the subsequent introduction of 50 μM AA along with these biomolecules led to marked fluorescence recovery, confirming that the system selectively responds to AA even in the presence of potentially interfering species. To elucidate the fluorescence enhancement mechanism, the absorbance spectra of the N,S-CDs/Cr(VI) system were analyzed at varying AA concentrations. Fig. 6 c illustrates that the absorbance of Cr(VI) at 364 nm progressively decreased and blue shifted to 330 nm as the AA concentration increased up to 90 μM. Moreover, a new absorption peak emerged at 567 nm and gradually intensified, indicating the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) upon interaction with AA. The presence of two distinct isosbestic points at 258 and 495 nm further supports the equilibrium between Cr(VI) and Cr(III) during the reduction process. These findings confirm that AA facilitates the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III), thereby alleviating the IFE and restoring the emission of N,S-CDs/Cr(VI) system. The proposed fluorescence “on-off-on” mechanism for the detection of Cr(VI) and AA using N,S-CDs is displayed in Scheme 1 .

The analytical performance of the as-synthesized N,S-CDs probe was evaluated by analyzing aqueous samples spiked with varying concentrations of Cr(VI) (50, 100, and 200 μM) using the standard addition method. As summarized in Table 3 , the method exhibited high accuracy and precision, with recovery rates ranging from 99 to 102 %, and relative standard deviations below 2.5 %. Similarly, Table 4 presents the detection results for AA in human serum samples and vitamin C tablet solutions using the N,S-CDs/Cr(VI) system. The samples were spiked with AA at concentrations of 50, 100, and 200 μM, achieving recovery rates of 97 to 103 % with RSD values below 2 %, confirming the probe’s reliability. These results align with expectations, further validating the feasibility of this method for real-world applications.

Furthermore, the fluorescence stability of N,S-CDs was assessed after storage at 4 °C for various time intervals, revealing that the fluorescence intensity remained at 95 % of its initial level even after three months (Fig.S6). This remarkable stability underscores the robustness and suitability of N,S-CDs as reliable fluorescence probes for biosensing applications.

In summary, N,S-CDs were successfully synthesized via a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method using citric acid and l-cysteine as precursors. The resulting CDs exhibited excellent water dispersibility, high stability in ionic environments, and strong fluorescence properties. The N,S-CDs offered excitation-independent and pH-dependent fluorescence behavior, enabling their use as dual-mode fluorescent probes. Under optimized conditions, the N,S-CDs enabled the selective and sensitive detection of Cr(VI) ions via a fluorescence “turn-off” mechanism predominantly governed by IFE. The probe also facilitated the “turn-on” detection of ascorbic acid (AA), based on the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III), which suppressed the IFE and restored fluorescence. The system showed high sensitivity with effective performance in detecting both Cr(VI) and AA in real samples. Despite these promising results, the sensing platform has some limitations. The detection performance may be affected by the presence of strong absorbance-overlapping species or environmental interferences in complex sample matrices. Additionally, the fluorescence response is pH-sensitive, which may require careful control in practical applications. Further studies focusing on probe selectivity under diverse field conditions and integration into portable detection formats are recommended to enhance the applicability of the sensing platform.

D. James Nelson: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. N.S.K. Gowthaman: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Bharathi Sinduja: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. M.G. Sethuraman: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Huang, H. Qiu, F. Zhu, S. Lu, Q. Xiao, Graphene quantum dots as on-off-on fluorescent probes for chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid, Microchim. Acta. 182 (2015) 1723–1731. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00604-015-1508-6.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dot membranes as fluorescent sensing platforms for Cr(VI) detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.M. Carrasco, I. García, L. Yate, R.T. Zaera, G. Cabanero, H.J. Grande, V. Ruiz, Graphene quantum dot membranes as fluorescent sensing platforms for Cr(VI) detection, Carbon N Y. 109 (2016) 658–665. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.08.038.
